visual_checks: Compare navigation menus across multiple page screenshots if available — do the menu items appear in the same relative order?
Check the primary navigation bar or menu — are the same links present in the same sequence on each page of the site?
Look at repeated navigation blocks such as breadcrumbs, sidebar menus, and footer navigation — are they positioned in the same location and in the same order across pages?
Check if navigation items are added or removed between pages — additions are acceptable as long as existing items maintain their relative order
Look at skip navigation links — if present on one page, are they consistently present and in the same position on other pages?
pass_conditions: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated across multiple pages appear in the same relative order each time they occur
Primary navigation menus maintain the same sequence of items across all pages (additional page-specific items may be inserted but do not disrupt the order of common items)
Footer navigation, sidebar menus, breadcrumbs, and other repeated navigation blocks maintain consistent ordering across pages
Skip navigation links appear in the same position on every page where they are present
The user can actively choose to change the navigation order (e.g., a customization feature), and the chosen order is then consistently maintained
fail_conditions: The primary navigation menu reorders its items between pages (e.g., 'Home | About | Contact' on one page becomes 'About | Home | Contact' on another)
Sidebar navigation links appear in a different sequence on different pages without the user initiating the change
Footer navigation rearranges its link order across different pages of the same site
A repeated set of action links (e.g., 'Edit | Delete | Share') appears in different orders in different contexts on the same site
Breadcrumb components change their structural position on the page inconsistently (e.g., above the heading on one page, below it on another)
na_conditions: The page is a standalone page with no repeated navigation shared with other pages (e.g., a single-page application with no multi-page navigation)
Only one page from the site was tested, making cross-page comparison impossible
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Only one page was captured, so navigation consistency across multiple pages cannot be evaluated
Screenshots do not show enough of the navigation to compare ordering across pages
Only one page was tested — consistent navigation requires comparing at least two pages from the same site. If this is a single-page review, set Not Applicable.
common_mistakes: Dynamically reordering navigation items based on page popularity or user analytics, causing different orderings on different pages
A CMS generating navigation in a different order for certain page templates
Mobile navigation menus that reorder items differently from the desktop navigation on the same pages
Sidebar navigation that sorts items alphabetically on some pages but by category on others
Search result page navigation that shuffles filter links compared to the main navigation

examples.pass: The top navigation bar reads 'Home | Products | Services | About | Contact' on every page of the site — on the Products page, a 'Categories' submenu is added but the top-level order remains the same
examples.fail: The top navigation reads 'Home | Products | About | Contact' on the homepage but 'Products | Home | Contact | About' on an interior page — the relative order of shared items has changed
examples.partial: Primary navigation is consistent across most pages, but two pages generated by a different CMS template have the footer links in a different order

plain_meaning: When navigation mechanisms (menus, breadcrumbs, sidebars) are repeated across multiple pages of a site, they must appear in the same relative order on each page. Users who have learned the layout on one page should find the same items in the same sequence on another. The user can opt into a reorder (for example via a personalization setting) and that is fine.
scope_applies_to: Primary navigation bars repeated across pages of a site
Secondary navigation, sidebars, or in-page section menus repeated across pages
Breadcrumb structures that appear on multiple pages
Skip link groups that appear on multiple pages
scope_does_not_apply_to: A single-page site or an isolated page not part of a set
Pages deliberately structured differently (for example a standalone landing page)
Reorderings initiated by the user (personalization, sorting, filtering)
Dynamic content area changes that are not part of navigation
Visual style differences as long as order is preserved
Hidden or collapsed states as long as the underlying order is consistent
borderline_calibration: Only the RELATIVE order of repeated items matters, not absolute position.
Adding or removing a page-specific item is acceptable as long as the rest stay in order.
Do not flag style or visual differences when ordering is consistent.
A single-page review with no sibling pages to compare yields Not Applicable or Not Enough Evidence.
